subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral (IMF Art IV, World Bank, ADB, WJP, RSF, TI, V-Dem) + Bangladeshi official (BBS, Bangladesh Bank) + Bangladeshi independent press (The Daily Star, Prothom Alo, bdnews24, TBS).

A post-revolution transition: the 2024 student uprising ousted the long-ruling prime minister; an interim government ran reforms with early-2026 polls producing an elected government - the transition resolving but contested.

Standout drags: a fragile, fraud-exposed banking sector, extreme climate vulnerability, and social-revenge volatility; offsets are a demographic dividend and a reform/press opening. Source-lean: MODERATE-HIGH Anglophone; Bengali-language reporting under-read.

economic.notes: A garment-export powerhouse with a reforming central bank, against a fraud-exposed banking sector and a thin tax base.

fiscal_state.notes: A moderate debt load but among the world's lowest tax takes (~7% of GDP) and IMF-program dependence with reserve stress. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

monetary_independence.notes: The central bank is rebuilding after 2024 - holding rates and ending FX slippage - from a historically constrained base. (Bangladesh Bank; IMF)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The taka is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Around 9% and easing slowly, with money-printing risk flagged. (BBS; IMF)

banking_stability.notes: Very high NPLs exposed by a post-2024 asset-quality review of looted banks, with a cleanup deadline - deep fragility. (TBS; World Bank)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: A top-tier garment-export powerhouse but with heavy single-sector concentration and a looming LDC-graduation tariff loss. (IGC; WTO)

institutional.notes: A weak base now reforming, with a press opening from a repressive era.

rule_of_law.notes: A weak rule-of-law base, though among the biggest recent improvers under interim-government reforms. (WJP 2025)

statistical_integrity.notes: Official-data quality is questioned, with prior politicisation. (IMF)

civil_service_capacity.notes: Weak and politicised, with reform commissions active. (UNDP; World Bank)

press_freedom.notes: A poor RSF standing but a real opening from the prior repressive era, still fragile (newsroom attacks). (RSF; Prothom Alo)

political_social.notes: A demographic dividend, against a contested transition, refugee-camp strain, and social-revenge volatility.

political_stability.notes: A 2024 student revolution ousted the long-ruling prime minister; an interim government ran reforms and early-2026 polls produced an elected government - the transition resolving but contested, with street unrest persisting. (Diplomat; IRI)

civil_liberties.notes: Improving from a repressive base, against mob-violence concerns. (Freedom House; RSF)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Hosts ~1.2M Rohingya refugees in strained camps with repatriation blocked; not a relocation destination. (Amnesty; UNHCR)

social_cohesion.notes: Political-revenge cycles, Hindu-minority attacks, and Islamist-resurgence concerns. (RRAG; Daily Star)

demographics.notes: A young workforce and replacement-level fertility - a genuine dividend, with high youth unemployment. (UNFPA)

geopolitical.notes: A foreign policy in flux, with Myanmar-border instability the main external exposure.

alliance_reliability.notes: Rebalancing - India ties strained post-2024, with China, Pakistan, and the US courted - in flux. (ORF; Chatham House)

conflict_involvement.notes: No external war, but Myanmar-border instability (an armed group holding the frontier) and Rohingya/Rakhine spillover. (CNN; bdnews24)

physical_practical.notes: Recent megaprojects, against extreme delta-climate vulnerability and near-nil catastrophe cover.

climate.notes: Among the world's most climate-vulnerable - cyclones, delta flooding, sea-level rise, and salinity on a low-lying delta. (World Bank; IPCC)

healthcare.notes: Low spend with improving basic indicators, uneven. (World Bank)

infrastructure.notes: Recent megaprojects delivered (a major bridge, a metro), but congested and power-strained. (TBS)

crime_safety.notes: Moderate, with post-revolution law-and-order deterioration. (Daily Star)

summary: A garment-export powerhouse with a genuine demographic dividend, navigating a post-revolution transition: the 2024 student uprising ousted the long-ruling prime minister, an interim government ran reforms, and early-2026 polls produced an elected government, with a real (if fragile) opening for the press and rule of law. The drags are a fraud-exposed banking sector with very high non-performing loans, a thin tax base under IMF dependence, extreme delta-climate vulnerability with near-nil catastrophe insurance, refugee-camp strain, and social-revenge volatility. The outcome distribution is balanced between reform upside and instability/climate downside.
